findings.summary: git show --name-only prints nothing for a merge commit, so [].every(isReviewRecord) was vacuously true: candidateShas walked through merges down the first-parent lineage and accepted another PR's review record, passing a merge of unreviewed code. Its other half: actions/checkout defaults to GitHub's synthetic merge ref, whose second parent carries the reviewed commit, so a correctly filed record would never be found. As wired it would have blocked every honest PR while passing merges of unreviewed code.

The selftest's git fixtures spread process.env, and git's cwd does not win over an ambient GIT_DIR. Running the selftest with GIT_DIR set wrote 8 commits into a repository the test does not own. The same hole existed in the gate's own git adapter, where candidateShas could have read history from a repository it was never pointed at. Observed, not theorised.

acceptedBy was checked for truthiness only, so an author could waive their own blocking finding with acceptedBy: 'me' while ADR-0052 claimed only the operator could. Waivers now require a name in reviews/OPERATORS, itself an implementation path.

scenarios/ matched wholesale, so a PR committing nothing but L4 evidence -- the project's own DoD workflow -- was forced to carry an adversarial code review. A gate that blocks honest work is a defect.
Two validateRecord rules (no findings array; finding without id/summary) had no selftest case.
Implementation path regexes were case-sensitive; a variant-cased path dodged the gate.
docs/V2-ROADMAP.md still instructed the reader to run the deleted /v2-review command.
REDESIGN could not be recorded, and the bidirectional mismatch check would have rejected an honest FIX-FIRST over major findings alone.
A non-string acceptedBy threw TypeError out of isOperator: fail-closed, but a stack trace instead of a finding.

Six of seven EXEMPT_PATHS entries were inert; deleting them reddened nothing, and the baseline case named 'reviews/ is exempt' passed for an unrelated reason. A list that read as protection and did nothing, with a test that read as coverage and proved nothing.
isImplementation did not normalise a leading ./ nor fail closed on a path containing a .. segment.
Three of four fixture git call sites passed -c user.email; the fourth did not, and CI runners have no global git identity. Green locally, red on CI.

The GIT_DIR strip added to the gate's own adapter had no test: reverting it reddened nothing. The assertion written for it checked the FIXTURE env object's keys, not the production adapter that decides which commits count as reviewed.

That fixture key-assertion was itself conditionally vacuous: it reddened only when the test runner's own environment already set GIT_DIR. Under env -i and on CI it delivered zero coverage while reading as proof of hermeticity. Found by the reviewer executing the exact regression it claimed to catch.

The behavioural replacement was ALSO vacuous: GIT_ENV was a const built at module load, before any test could set GIT_DIR, so a snapshot could not leak a variable that did not yet exist and the guard could not fail in-process. Two vacuous guards stacked, both written to close a vacuity. The env is now built per call, and each guard reddens exactly its own regression, independent of the ambient environment.

candidateShas guards a parentless-root rev-parse throw, but the guard has no fixture: it is unreachable through the wired adapter, since base is always a merge-base and therefore an ancestor of HEAD. Disclosed rather than covered by a fixture written only to move a number. The reviewer's exhaustive 26-rule mutation sweep confirms this is the ONLY rule whose revert reddens no case.
